# Vectors — Core Concepts

---

## 1. Scalar Product (Dot Product)

### What is the dot product?

One of the ways to "multiply" two vectors. Given two vectors as input, a single **number (scalar)** comes out — which is why it is called the scalar product.

::: {.callout-note title="Why do we study this? — Uses of the dot product"}
The dot product is used in exactly two situations:

1. **Finding the angle between two vectors** — $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\vec{a}\cdot\vec{b}}{|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|}$
2. **Checking whether two vectors are perpendicular** — if the dot product $= 0$, they are perpendicular

The key point is that you can calculate the angle directly from the components (coordinates) alone. Without the dot product, there is no way to find the angle between two lines or the distance from a point to a line.
:::

**Intuition:** The more two vectors point in the same direction, the larger the dot product. If they are exactly perpendicular it is 0; if they point in opposite directions it is negative.

$$\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = |\vec{a}||\vec{b}|\cos\theta$$

### Geometric Meaning

**Dot product = magnitude of one vector × projection of the other onto it**

$$\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = |\vec{a}| \underbrace{|\vec{b}|\cos\theta}_{\text{projection of } \vec{b} \text{ onto } \vec{a}}$$

The scalar projection of $\vec{b}$ onto $\vec{a}$ is $|\vec{b}|\cos\theta$ — how much of $\vec{b}$ lies in the direction of $\vec{a}$.

**Sign tells you the angle:**

| $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b}$ | $\cos\theta$ | Angle | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| $> 0$ | $> 0$ | $\theta < 90°$ | Vectors point in roughly the same direction |
| $= 0$ | $= 0$ | $\theta = 90°$ | Perpendicular |
| $< 0$ | $< 0$ | $\theta > 90°$ | Vectors point in roughly opposite directions |

::: {.callout-tip title="KEY"}
- $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = \vec{b} \cdot \vec{a}$ **(commutative)** and $\vec{a} \cdot (\vec{b} + \vec{c}) = \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} + \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}$ **(distributive)**
- $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = 0$ if $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ are **perpendicular**
- $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = |\vec{a}||\vec{b}|$ if $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$ are **parallel**; in particular, $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a} = |\vec{a}|^2$
:::

**Unit vectors** $i, j, k$:

$$i \cdot i = 1, \qquad i \cdot j = 0$$

**Column matrix form:**

$$\begin{pmatrix}a_1\\a_2\\a_3\end{pmatrix} \cdot \begin{pmatrix}b_1\\b_2\\b_3\end{pmatrix} = a_1b_1 + a_2b_2 + a_3b_3$$

::: {.callout-tip title="KEY — Finding the angle"}
$$\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = |\vec{a}||\vec{b}|\cos\theta = a_1b_1 + a_2b_2 + a_3b_3$$

$$\cos\theta = \frac{a_1b_1 + a_2b_2 + a_3b_3}{|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|}$$
:::

---

<details>
<summary>**Ex 1 — Angle between two vectors**</summary>

Given $\vec{a} = -i+j+3k$ and $\vec{b} = 7i-2j+2k$, find the angle between $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$.

$$\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = (-1)(7) + (1)(-2) + (3)(2) = -7 - 2 + 6 = -3$$

$$|\vec{a}| = \sqrt{1+1+9} = \sqrt{11}, \qquad |\vec{b}| = \sqrt{49+4+4} = \sqrt{57}$$

$$\cos\theta = \frac{-3}{\sqrt{11}\cdot\sqrt{57}} = \frac{-3}{\sqrt{627}}$$

$$\boxed{\theta = \arccos\!\left(\frac{-3}{\sqrt{627}}\right) \approx 96.9°}$$

</details>

---

<details>
<summary>**Ex 2 — Perpendicular vectors: find $\lambda$**</summary>

Given $\vec{a} = 2i-6j+k$ and $\vec{b} = 5i+2j+\lambda k$ are perpendicular, find $\lambda$.

If perpendicular: $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = 0$

$$(2)(5) + (-6)(2) + (1)(\lambda) = 0$$

$$10 - 12 + \lambda = 0$$

$$\boxed{\lambda = 2}$$

</details>

---

## 2. Line Equation in 3D

| Equation Type | 2D | 3D |
|---|---|---|
| **Vector** | $\vec{r} = \vec{a} + \lambda\vec{b} = \begin{pmatrix}a_1\\a_2\end{pmatrix} + \lambda\begin{pmatrix}b_1\\b_2\end{pmatrix}$ | $\vec{r} = \begin{pmatrix}a_1\\a_2\\a_3\end{pmatrix} + \lambda\begin{pmatrix}b_1\\b_2\\b_3\end{pmatrix}$ |
| **Parametric** | $\begin{cases}x = a_1 + \lambda b_1\\y = a_2 + \lambda b_2\end{cases}$ | $\begin{cases}x = a_1 + \lambda b_1\\y = a_2 + \lambda b_2\\z = a_3 + \lambda b_3\end{cases}$ |
| **Cartesian** | $\dfrac{x-a_1}{b_1} = \dfrac{y-a_2}{b_2}$ | $\dfrac{x-a_1}{b_1} = \dfrac{y-a_2}{b_2} = \dfrac{z-a_3}{b_3}$ |

::: {.callout-note title="Note"}
- $\vec{a}$: **position vector** of a point on the line
- $\vec{b}$: **direction vector** of the line
- $\lambda$: scalar **parameter**
:::

<details>
<summary>**Connection between the vector equation and $y = mx + c$**</summary>

In 2D, the vector equation $\vec{r} = \begin{pmatrix}x_0\\y_0\end{pmatrix} + \lambda\begin{pmatrix}p\\q\end{pmatrix}$ in parametric form is:

$$x = x_0 + \lambda p, \qquad y = y_0 + \lambda q$$

Eliminating $\lambda$ gives $\lambda = \dfrac{x-x_0}{p} = \dfrac{y-y_0}{q}$, so:

$$y = \underbrace{\frac{q}{p}}_{m}\,x + \underbrace{\!\left(y_0 - \frac{q}{p}x_0\right)}_{c}$$

That is, **the ratio of the direction vector components becomes the gradient directly**:

$$m = \frac{q}{p} = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$$

**Example:** $\vec{r} = \begin{pmatrix}1\\3\end{pmatrix} + \lambda\begin{pmatrix}2\\6\end{pmatrix}$ → $m = \dfrac{6}{2} = 3$, $c = 3 - 3(1) = 0$ → $y = 3x$

::: {.callout-note}
In 3D, the $y = mx+c$ form is not possible. With three unknowns, a single equation cannot uniquely define a line. In 3D you must use the vector form or the Cartesian form $\dfrac{x-a_1}{b_1} = \dfrac{y-a_2}{b_2} = \dfrac{z-a_3}{b_3}$.
:::

</details>

---

<details>
<summary>**$\vec{a}$와 $\vec{b}$는 표기가 같은데 뭐가 달라?**</summary>

둘 다 칼럼벡터 $\begin{pmatrix}x\\y\\z\end{pmatrix}$ 형태라 표기는 완전히 같아. 차이는 **의미**야.

| | 위치벡터 $\vec{a}$ | 방향벡터 $\vec{b}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 의미 | 원점 O에서 직선 위 한 점까지 | 직선의 방향 (크기·방향만) |
| 위치 | 원점에 묶여 있음 | 어디서 시작하든 상관없음 |

**핵심 차이 — 스케일을 바꾸면?**

- $\vec{a}$를 $2\vec{a}$로 바꾸면 → 다른 점을 가리키므로 **다른 직선**
- $\vec{b}$를 $2\vec{b}$로 바꿔도 → 방향만 표현하는 거라 **같은 직선**

즉 $\vec{b}$는 방향 정보만 담고 있어서 스칼라배해도 동일한 직선이야.

</details>

```{python}
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 4.8))
ax.set_xlim(-0.3, 11); ax.set_ylim(-0.8, 4.0)
ax.set_aspect('equal'); ax.axis('off')

BLUE  = '#2563EB'
RED   = '#DC2626'
GREEN = '#16A34A'
PURP  = '#7C3AED'
GREY  = '#9CA3AF'

def arr(ax, x0, y0, dx, dy, color, lw=2.2, ms=14, ls='solid', zorder=3):
    ax.annotate('', xy=(x0+dx, y0+dy), xytext=(x0, y0),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color=color, lw=lw,
                                mutation_scale=ms, linestyle=ls),
                zorder=zorder)

O = np.array([0.0, 0.0])
A = np.array([3.2, 1.4])      # position vector a points here
b = np.array([1.6, 0.55])     # direction vector
P = A + 2.0 * b               # general point at lambda=2

# extended line
t = np.linspace(-1.1, 3.5, 300)
lpts = np.array([A + ti*b for ti in t])
ax.plot(lpts[:,0], lpts[:,1], color=GREY, lw=1.3, ls='--', zorder=1)

# lambda tick marks
perp = np.array([-b[1], b[0]]) / np.linalg.norm(b) * 0.14
for lv, label, voffset in [(-1, r'$\lambda{=}{-1}$', -0.30),
                             ( 0, r'$\lambda{=}0\ (A)$', -0.30),
                             ( 1, r'$\lambda{=}1$',      -0.30),
                             ( 2, r'$\lambda{=}2$',      -0.30)]:
    Pt = A + lv*b
    ax.plot([Pt[0]-perp[0], Pt[0]+perp[0]],
            [Pt[1]-perp[1], Pt[1]+perp[1]], color=GREY, lw=1.2, zorder=2)
    ax.text(Pt[0], Pt[1]+voffset, label,
            ha='center', va='top', fontsize=8, color=GREY)

# O
ax.plot(*O, 'ko', ms=6, zorder=6)
ax.text(O[0]-0.05, O[1]-0.32, '$O$', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold', ha='center')

# a : O → A  (blue)
arr(ax, *O, *(A-O), BLUE, lw=2.4)
ax.plot(*A, 'o', color=BLUE, ms=7, zorder=6)
ax.text(A[0]-0.08, A[1]+0.2, '$A$', fontsize=12, color=BLUE, fontweight='bold')
ax.text(A[0]/2 - 0.42, A[1]/2 + 0.15, r'$\vec{a}$',
        fontsize=15, color=BLUE, fontweight='bold')

# b : A → A+b  (green)
arr(ax, *A, *b, GREEN, lw=2.5, ms=13)
mid_b = A + b*0.5
ax.text(mid_b[0] + 0.05, mid_b[1] + 0.22, r'$\vec{b}$',
        fontsize=15, color=GREEN, fontweight='bold')

# lambda*b : A → P  (purple dashed)
arr(ax, *A, *(P-A), PURP, lw=1.8, ms=12, ls='dashed', zorder=4)
mid_lb = A + (P-A)*0.5
ax.text(mid_lb[0] + 0.05, mid_lb[1] + 0.22, r'$\lambda\vec{b}$',
        fontsize=14, color=PURP, fontweight='bold')

# r : O → P  (red)
arr(ax, *O, *(P-O), RED, lw=2.2, zorder=2)
ax.plot(*P, 'o', color=RED, ms=7, zorder=6)
ax.text(P[0]+0.1, P[1]+0.2, '$P$', fontsize=12, color=RED, fontweight='bold')
ax.text((O[0]+P[0])/2 + 0.35, (O[1]+P[1])/2 + 0.28, r'$\vec{r}$',
        fontsize=15, color=RED, fontweight='bold')

# equation box (top right)
ax.text(9.0, 3.3, r'$\vec{r} = \vec{a} + \lambda\vec{b}$',
        fontsize=15, ha='center',
        bbox=dict(boxstyle='round,pad=0.55', facecolor='#EFF6FF',
                  edgecolor=BLUE, lw=1.8))

# legend — English only (avoids font rendering issues)
legend = [
    (BLUE,  r'$\vec{a}$ : position vector,  $O \to A$'),
    (GREEN, r'$\vec{b}$ : direction vector  (free — can start anywhere)'),
    (PURP,  r'$\lambda\vec{b}$ : scaled step from $A$ to $P$'),
    (RED,   r'$\vec{r}$ : position vector of $P$,  $O \to P$'),
]
lx, ly0 = 7.2, 2.55
for i, (c, txt) in enumerate(legend):
    ax.plot(lx, ly0 - i*0.62, 'o', color=c, ms=7, zorder=5)
    ax.text(lx+0.22, ly0 - i*0.62, txt, fontsize=9.2, color=c, va='center')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

<details>
<summary>**Cartesian form — 왜 저렇게 생겼나?**</summary>

Parametric 각 식에서 $\lambda$를 표현하면:

$$x = a_1 + \lambda b_1 \implies \lambda = \frac{x-a_1}{b_1}$$

$$y = a_2 + \lambda b_2 \implies \lambda = \frac{y-a_2}{b_2}$$

$$z = a_3 + \lambda b_3 \implies \lambda = \frac{z-a_3}{b_3}$$

세 식이 **모두 같은 $\lambda$**이므로 등호로 연결 → $\lambda$ 소거:

$$\frac{x-a_1}{b_1} = \frac{y-a_2}{b_2} = \frac{z-a_3}{b_3}$$

즉 Cartesian form은 **Parametric에서 $\lambda$를 없앤 것**. $x, y, z$만으로 직선의 조건을 표현한 형태야.

---

**주의 — 방향벡터 성분이 0일 때**

예: 방향벡터가 $(2, 0, 1)$이면 $y$ 성분이 $0$이라 $\dfrac{y-a_2}{0}$ 꼴이 나와.

이건 "분모가 0" 이 아니라 **$y = a_2$ (상수)** 라는 뜻으로 따로 적어줘야 해:

$$\frac{x-a_1}{2} = \frac{z-a_3}{1}, \quad y = a_2$$

---

**실전에서는?**

A-Level에서 Cartesian form이 직접 요구되는 경우는 드물어. Vector/Parametric이 훨씬 많이 쓰이고, 교점·수선 등 계산도 Parametric으로 해.

</details>

<details>
<summary>**핵심 아이디어 — 왜 이렇게 쓰나?**</summary>

직선은 **"직선 위의 한 점 + 방향"** 만 알면 완전히 결정돼.

$$\vec{r} = \underbrace{\vec{a}}_{\text{직선 위 한 점}} + \lambda\underbrace{\vec{b}}_{\text{방향 벡터}}$$

$\lambda$를 바꾸면 직선 위 모든 점을 다 찍을 수 있어. ($\lambda = 0$이면 점 $A$ 자체, $\lambda = 1$이면 $\vec{a}+\vec{b}$, 음수면 반대 방향.)

**3가지 표현은 모두 같은 직선이야 — 형태만 다를 뿐:**

$$\text{Vector} \xrightarrow{\text{성분별 분리}} \text{Parametric} \xrightarrow{\lambda \text{ 소거}} \text{Cartesian}$$

</details>

<details>
<summary>**Vector → Parametric → Cartesian 변환 방법**</summary>

**Step 1. Vector → Parametric** (성분별로 분리)

$$\vec{r} = \begin{pmatrix}3\\2\\-5\end{pmatrix} + t\begin{pmatrix}1\\-6\\-2\end{pmatrix} \implies \begin{cases}x = 3+t\\y = 2-6t\\z = -5-2t\end{cases}$$

**Step 2. Parametric → Cartesian** (각 식에서 $\lambda$ 구해 연결)

각 식에서 $t$를 표현하면: $t = x-3$, $t = \dfrac{y-2}{-6}$, $t = \dfrac{z+5}{-2}$

모두 같은 $t$이므로:

$$\frac{x-3}{1} = \frac{y-2}{-6} = \frac{z+5}{-2}$$

> **주의:** 방향벡터 성분이 $0$이면 $\dfrac{y-1}{0}$ 꼴이 나오는데, 이는 $y=1$ (그 좌표가 상수)를 의미해. (Ex 6 참고)

</details>

---

<details>
<summary>**Ex 3 — Line through a point, parallel to a vector**</summary>

Find a vector equation of the straight line which passes through $A(3,4,4)$ and is parallel to $(7i-4k)$.

$$\boxed{\vec{r} = \begin{pmatrix}3\\4\\4\end{pmatrix} + \lambda\begin{pmatrix}7\\0\\-4\end{pmatrix}}$$

</details>

---

<details>
<summary>**Ex 4 — Line through two points**</summary>

Find a vector equation of the line through $A(4,5,-1)$ and $B(6,3,2)$.

Direction vector: $\overrightarrow{AB} = B - A = \begin{pmatrix}2\\-2\\3\end{pmatrix}$

$$\boxed{\vec{r} = \begin{pmatrix}4\\5\\-1\end{pmatrix} + \lambda\begin{pmatrix}2\\-2\\3\end{pmatrix}}$$

</details>

---

<details>
<summary>**Ex 5 — Parametric form from vector equation**</summary>

Express in parametric form: $\vec{r} = (3i+2j-5k) + t(i-6j-2k)$

$$\begin{cases}x = 3 + t\\y = 2 - 6t\\z = -5 - 2t\end{cases}$$

</details>

---

<details>
<summary>**Ex 6 — Cartesian equation from two points**</summary>

Find a Cartesian equation of the line through $A(2,1,9)$ and $B(4,1,-8)$.

Direction vector: $\overrightarrow{AB} = \begin{pmatrix}2\\0\\-17\end{pmatrix}$

$$\frac{x-2}{2} = \frac{y-1}{0} = \frac{z-9}{-17}$$

Note: $\dfrac{y-1}{0}$ means $y = 1$ (constant — direction component is zero).

</details>

---

## 3. Types of Questions

```{python}
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.gridspec import GridSpec

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(14, 6.5))
gs = GridSpec(3, 4, figure=fig,
              height_ratios=[0.55, 3.0, 3.0],
              width_ratios=[0.85, 3.4, 3.4, 3.4],
              hspace=0, wspace=0)

GREY   = '#6B7280'
BLACK  = '#111827'
RED    = '#DC2626'
HDR_BG = '#F3F4F6'
BDR    = '#9CA3AF'

def style_header(ax, text, rotation=0, fs=12):
    ax.set_facecolor(HDR_BG)
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
    for sp in ax.spines.values():
        sp.set_edgecolor(BDR); sp.set_linewidth(1.2)
    ax.text(0.5, 0.5, text, ha='center', va='center',
            fontsize=fs, fontweight='bold',
            transform=ax.transAxes, rotation=rotation)

def style_cell(ax, bg='#FFFFFF'):
    ax.set_facecolor(bg)
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
    for sp in ax.spines.values():
        sp.set_edgecolor(BDR); sp.set_linewidth(1.0)
    ax.set_xlim(0, 4); ax.set_ylim(0, 3)

def cell_title(ax, text, color):
    ax.text(0.5, 0.96, text, ha='center', va='top', fontsize=9.5,
            fontweight='bold', transform=ax.transAxes, color=color)

# ── Headers ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
style_header(fig.add_subplot(gs[0, 0]), '')
style_header(fig.add_subplot(gs[0, 1]), 'Point')
style_header(fig.add_subplot(gs[0, 2:4]), 'Line')
style_header(fig.add_subplot(gs[1, 0]), 'Point', rotation=90)
style_header(fig.add_subplot(gs[2, 0]), 'Line',  rotation=90)

# ── ① Point × Point : distance ──────────────────────────────────────────────
ax1 = fig.add_subplot(gs[1, 1])
style_cell(ax1, '#F0F9FF')
cell_title(ax1, '① distance', '#1E40AF')
A = np.array([0.75, 0.85]); B = np.array([2.95, 2.0])
ax1.plot(*A, 'ko', ms=7, zorder=5)
ax1.text(A[0]-0.32, A[1]-0.36, '$A$', fontsize=12)
ax1.plot(*B, 'ko', ms=7, zorder=5)
ax1.text(B[0]+0.1, B[1]+0.1, '$B$', fontsize=12)
ax1.plot([A[0],B[0]], [A[1],B[1]], 'k--', lw=1.6)
mid = (A+B)/2
ax1.text(mid[0]+0.14, mid[1]+0.1, '$d$', fontsize=13, style='italic')

# ── Point × Line : empty ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ax_e = fig.add_subplot(gs[1, 2:4])
style_cell(ax_e, '#FAFAFA')
ax_e.text(0.5, 0.5, '—', ha='center', va='center', fontsize=16,
          color=GREY, transform=ax_e.transAxes)

# ── ② Line × Point : distance (foot of ⊥) ───────────────────────────────────
ax2 = fig.add_subplot(gs[2, 1])
style_cell(ax2, '#F0F9FF')
cell_title(ax2, '② distance (foot of ⊥)', '#1E40AF')
ldir = np.array([1.0, 0.18]); ldir /= np.linalg.norm(ldir)
lstart = np.array([0.2, 0.82]); lend = lstart + 3.6*ldir
ax2.annotate('', xy=lend, xytext=lstart,
             arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color=BLACK, lw=1.4, mutation_scale=11))
ax2.text(lend[0]-0.12, lend[1]+0.16, '$l$', fontsize=11)
A2 = lstart + 0.7*ldir
ax2.plot(*A2, 'ko', ms=5)
ax2.text(A2[0]-0.26, A2[1]-0.3, '$A$', fontsize=10)
P2 = np.array([1.9, 2.35])
ax2.plot(*P2, 'ko', ms=5)
ax2.text(P2[0]+0.1, P2[1]+0.1, '$P$', fontsize=10)
t_val = np.dot(P2-lstart, ldir); F2 = lstart + t_val*ldir
ax2.plot(*F2, 's', color=GREY, ms=6, zorder=5)
ax2.text(F2[0]+0.1, F2[1]-0.3, '$F$', fontsize=10, color=GREY)
ax2.plot([P2[0],F2[0]], [P2[1],F2[1]], '--', color=RED, lw=1.5)
fp = (P2-F2)/np.linalg.norm(P2-F2); ra=0.11
c1=F2+ldir*ra; c2=c1+fp*ra; c3=F2+fp*ra
ax2.plot([c1[0],c2[0],c3[0]], [c1[1],c2[1],c3[1]], 'k-', lw=1)
ax2.text((P2[0]+F2[0])/2+0.13, (P2[1]+F2[1])/2,
         '$d$', fontsize=13, style='italic', color=RED)

# ── ③④ Line × Line : intersection & angle ───────────────────────────────────
ax3 = fig.add_subplot(gs[2, 2])
style_cell(ax3, '#F0FFF4')
cell_title(ax3, '③ intersection   ④ angle', '#166534')
l1s=np.array([0.2,0.4]); l1e=np.array([3.6,2.6])
ax3.annotate('', xy=l1e, xytext=l1s,
             arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color=BLACK, lw=1.4, mutation_scale=11))
ax3.text(l1e[0]+0.05, l1e[1]+0.05, '$l_1$', fontsize=10)
l2s=np.array([0.2,2.7]); l2e=np.array([3.6,0.3])
ax3.annotate('', xy=l2e, xytext=l2s,
             arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color=BLACK, lw=1.4, mutation_scale=11))
ax3.text(l2e[0]+0.05, l2e[1]-0.2, '$l_2$', fontsize=10)
d1=l1e-l1s; d2=l2e-l2s
A_m=np.array([[d1[0],-d2[0]],[d1[1],-d2[1]]]); b_v=l2s-l1s
ts=np.linalg.solve(A_m, b_v); Pint=l1s+ts[0]*d1
ax3.plot(*Pint, 'ko', ms=6, zorder=5)
ax3.text(Pint[0]+0.12, Pint[1]+0.14, '$P$', fontsize=10)

# ── ⑤ Line × Line : skew distance (FP) ─────────────────────────────────────
ax4 = fig.add_subplot(gs[2, 3])
style_cell(ax4, '#FFF7ED')
cell_title(ax4, '⑤ skew distance  (FP only)', '#9A3412')
l1s4=np.array([0.2,1.9]); l1e4=np.array([3.7,1.4])
ax4.annotate('', xy=l1e4, xytext=l1s4,
             arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color=BLACK, lw=1.4, mutation_scale=11))
ax4.text(l1e4[0]+0.05, l1e4[1]+0.1, '$l_1$', fontsize=10)
l2s4=np.array([0.4,0.4]); l2e4=np.array([2.9,2.75])
ax4.annotate('', xy=l2e4, xytext=l2s4,
             arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color=BLACK, lw=1.4, mutation_scale=11))
ax4.text(l2e4[0]+0.05, l2e4[1]+0.05, '$l_2$', fontsize=10)
m1_4=l1s4+0.50*(l1e4-l1s4); m2_4=l2s4+0.45*(l2e4-l2s4)
ax4.plot([m1_4[0],m2_4[0]], [m1_4[1],m2_4[1]], '--', color=RED, lw=1.8)
ax4.text((m1_4[0]+m2_4[0])/2+0.14, (m1_4[1]+m2_4[1])/2,
         '$d$', fontsize=13, style='italic', color=RED)

plt.show()
```

### ① Distance between two points

$$d = |\vec{b} - \vec{a}| = \sqrt{(b_1-a_1)^2+(b_2-a_2)^2+(b_3-a_3)^2}$$

<details>
<summary>**Ex — Distance between two points**</summary>

Find the distance between two points whose position vectors are $\vec{a} = 5\mathbf{i} - 2\mathbf{j}$ and $\vec{b} = 4\mathbf{i} - \mathbf{j} + 3\mathbf{k}$.

$$\vec{b} - \vec{a} = \begin{pmatrix}4-5\\-1+2\\3-0\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}-1\\1\\3\end{pmatrix}$$

$$d = \sqrt{(-1)^2+1^2+3^2} = \sqrt{1+1+9} = \boxed{\sqrt{11}}$$

</details>

### ③ Intersection of two lines

Set $l_1 = l_2$ using parameters $\lambda$ and $\mu$. Solve the system (2 equations, 2 unknowns). Substitute back to verify the third equation — if inconsistent, lines are **skew**.

### ④ Angle between two lines

Use direction vectors $\vec{b}_1$ and $\vec{b}_2$ only — position vectors $\vec{a}_1$, $\vec{a}_2$ are irrelevant:

$$\cos\theta = \frac{|\vec{b}_1 \cdot \vec{b}_2|}{|\vec{b}_1||\vec{b}_2|}$$

::: {.callout-note title="왜 방향벡터만 쓰나?"}
**이유 1 — 각도는 위치가 아니라 방향의 성질이다.**
$\vec{r} = \vec{a} + \lambda\vec{b}$에서 $\vec{a}$는 직선의 위치, $\vec{b}$는 직선의 방향이다. 두 직선을 아무리 평행이동해도 이루는 각도는 변하지 않으므로 $\vec{b}$만 사용한다.

**이유 2 — 절댓값을 취하는 이유.**
직선은 양방향으로 뻗어있어서 $\vec{b}$와 $-\vec{b}$는 같은 직선이다. 내적이 음수(둔각)로 나와도 직선 사이의 각도는 항상 **예각**으로 정의하므로 $|\vec{b}_1 \cdot \vec{b}_2|$로 절댓값을 취한다.
:::

### ② Distance from a point to a line (foot of perpendicular)

Let $F$ be the foot of perpendicular from point $P$ to line $l: \vec{r} = \vec{a} + \lambda\vec{b}$.

1. Write $F = \vec{a} + \lambda\vec{b}$ (unknown $\lambda$)
2. $\overrightarrow{PF} \cdot \vec{b} = 0$ (perpendicularity condition) → solve for $\lambda$
3. Find $F$, then $d = |\overrightarrow{PF}|$

---

### Skew Lines — 개념

3D에서 두 직선의 관계는 **네 가지**야:

| 관계 | 조건 |
|---|---|
| **Intersecting** | 방향벡터가 평행하지 않고, 공통점이 존재함 |
| **Skew** | 방향벡터가 평행하지 않고, 공통점도 없음 — 3D에서만 존재 |
| **Parallel** | 방향벡터가 scalar multiple, 만나지 않음 |
| **Coincident** | 방향벡터가 scalar multiple, 같은 직선 |

**Skew lines**는 아예 다른 평면 위에 있어서 어디서도 만나지 않아. 각도가 달라서가 아니라 **공간적으로 엇갈린** 거야.

### 판별 흐름도

$$\mathbf{b_1} = k\,\mathbf{b_2} \text{ ?}$$

```
방향벡터가 평행?  (b₁ = k·b₂)
│
├─ YES → line 1의 점 a₁을 line 2에 대입해서 t가 나오나?
│         ├─ YES → Coincident (같은 선)
│         └─ NO  → Parallel (평행, 절대 안 만남)
│
└─ NO  → 연립방정식으로 λ, μ 풀기 (2개 식으로)
          ├─ 3번째 식도 성립 → Intersect (교점 존재)
          └─ 3번째 식 불성립 → Skew
```

::: {.callout-important title="Parallel 판별 주의"}
방향벡터가 **완전히 같을 필요 없다** — scalar multiple이면 충분.

$\mathbf{b_1} = \begin{pmatrix}2\\4\\6\end{pmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b_2} = \begin{pmatrix}1\\2\\3\end{pmatrix}$ → $\mathbf{b_1} = 2\,\mathbf{b_2}$ 이므로 parallel (또는 coincident).

Coincident인지 확인하려면: $\mathbf{a_1}$을 $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{a_2} + t\mathbf{b_2}$에 대입해서 실수 $t$가 존재하면 coincident.
:::

**③에서 교점을 구할 때 판별하는 법:**

연립방정식을 세워서 $\lambda$, $\mu$를 구한 뒤 **세 번째 방정식에 대입해 검증**해야 해.

- 세 번째 식이 성립 → **intersecting** (교점 존재)
- 세 번째 식이 불성립 → **skew** (교점 없음)

::: {.callout-note title="⑤ Skew distance — FP only"}
두 skew 직선 사이의 최단거리 공식은 **FP (Further Pure)** 과정이야. P3/P4에서는 skew 여부를 **판별**하는 것까지만 나와 — 거리 계산은 안 해도 돼.
:::

---

<details>
<summary>**Ex 7 — Intersection point of two lines**</summary>

$$l_1: \vec{r} = \begin{pmatrix}3\\8\\-2\end{pmatrix} + \lambda\begin{pmatrix}2\\-1\\3\end{pmatrix}, \qquad l_2: \vec{r} = \begin{pmatrix}7\\4\\3\end{pmatrix} + \mu\begin{pmatrix}2\\1\\4\end{pmatrix}$$

Setting equal: $3+2\lambda = 7+2\mu,\; 8-\lambda = 4+\mu,\; -2+3\lambda = 3+4\mu$

From equations 1 and 2: $\lambda = 2,\; \mu = -1$.

Check equation 3: $-2+6 = 4$ and $3-4 = -1$ — **not equal**, so lines are **skew**.

</details>

---

<details>
<summary>**Ex 8 — Acute angle between two lines**</summary>

$$l_1: \vec{r} = \begin{pmatrix}2\\1\\1\end{pmatrix} + \lambda\begin{pmatrix}3\\-8\\-1\end{pmatrix}, \qquad l_2: \vec{r} = \begin{pmatrix}7\\4\\1\end{pmatrix} + \mu\begin{pmatrix}2\\2\\3\end{pmatrix}$$

$$\vec{b}_1 \cdot \vec{b}_2 = (3)(2)+(-8)(2)+(-1)(3) = 6-16-3 = -13$$

$$|\vec{b}_1| = \sqrt{9+64+1} = \sqrt{74}, \qquad |\vec{b}_2| = \sqrt{4+4+9} = \sqrt{17}$$

$$\cos\theta = \frac{|-13|}{\sqrt{74}\cdot\sqrt{17}} = \frac{13}{\sqrt{1258}}$$

$$\boxed{\theta \approx 68.6°}$$

</details>

---

<details>
<summary>**Ex 9 — Distance from point to line**</summary>

Find the distance from $P(4,1,-1)$ to $l: \vec{r} = (4i-4i+3k) + t(i-2j+2k)$.

Let $F = \begin{pmatrix}4+t\\-4-2t\\3+2t\end{pmatrix}$. Then $\overrightarrow{PF} = \begin{pmatrix}t\\-5-2t\\4+2t\end{pmatrix}$.

Perpendicularity: $\overrightarrow{PF} \cdot \begin{pmatrix}1\\-2\\2\end{pmatrix} = 0$

$$t + 10 + 4t + 8 + 4t = 0 \implies 9t = -18 \implies t = -2$$

$$F = \begin{pmatrix}2\\0\\-1\end{pmatrix}, \qquad \overrightarrow{PF} = \begin{pmatrix}-2\\-1\\0\end{pmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{d = \sqrt{4+1+0} = \sqrt{5}}$$

</details>
